title: Tanacetum carneum
taxonomy: Tanacetum carneum is classified in the family Asteraceae, which is one of the largest and most widespread families of flowering plants. It is a member of the subfamily Asteroideae and the tribe Anthemideae. The genus Tanacetum is commonly known as tansy, and it includes about 80 species of flowering plants. Tanacetum carneum is native to certain regions and is closely related to other species within the same genus. The classification of Tanacetum carneum helps scientists understand its evolutionary relationships and characteristics.
uses: Tanacetum carneum is known to have various medicinal properties, including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects. The plant has been traditionally used to treat skin conditions such as acne, wounds, and eczema. Its anti-inflammatory properties also make it useful in reducing pain and swelling. Additionally, Tanacetum carneum has been studied for its potential to reduce stress and anxiety due to its adaptogenic properties. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Tanacetum carneum for medicinal purposes.

parts: Tanacetum carneum is also known as Pyrethrum, and its medicinal parts include the flowers and leaves. The flowers are rich in pyrethrins, which have insecticidal and antiseptic properties. preparations: Tanacetum carneum herbal preparations are not well-documented, but some sources suggest that the plant, also known as Mountain Tarragon, has been used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments. Tanacetum vulgare is a perennial herb native to Europe and Asia, characterized by its fern-like foliage and yellow or white flowers. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and carminative properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, menstrual cramps, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Tanacetum vulgare include sesquiterpene lactones, particularly tanacetone, and volatile oils, such as borneol and camphor. Tanacetum vulgare is commonly used to make herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to treat digestive issues, headaches, and menstrual disorders.
